name: Panama Canal Drought 2023-2024
description: An infrastructure notebook showing how drought conditions in Panama constrained canal transits, changed ship scheduling, and made the watershed itself part of the shipping story.

sides.name: Canal and watershed system
sides.description: The canal operator and watershed are inseparable in a drought year because water management becomes the core operational question.

subUnits.name: Panama Canal Authority
subUnits.description:
The authority had to translate low water into draft limits, transit caps, booking rules, and public guidance for global shipping.
- Role: Water-constrained transit manager
- Geographic anchor: Centered on the canal corridor itself, with special operational attention on Gatun Lake and the lock system.
- Why it matters: It was the actor turning climate stress into operational limits that shippers could not ignore.
- What to watch: Lake levels, transit caps, booking changes, and water-saving measures.

subUnits.name: Gatun Lake watershed system
subUnits.description:
The watershed and lake levels determined how much water could be used for ship lockages and, by extension, how much transit capacity remained.
- Role: Hydrologic constraint
- Geographic anchor: Focused on Gatun Lake and the broader catchment feeding the canal.
- Why it matters: It is the environmental engine behind every operational restriction described in the notebook.
- What to watch: Rainfall deficits, lake elevation, and how water-saving measures change daily operations.

sides.name: Shipping demand
sides.description: Ships did not simply disappear when restrictions tightened; they queued, bid for slots, rerouted, or turned toward intermodal workarounds.

subUnits.name: Container carriers
subUnits.description:
Container lines needed schedule reliability, making them highly sensitive to queue length and slot uncertainty.
- Role: Schedule-sensitive canal demand
- Geographic anchor: Visible in Atlantic and Pacific queue areas and in the booking competition for restricted slots.
- Why it matters: Carrier behavior is the clearest operational signal that canal restrictions have crossed into a network disruption story.
- What to watch: Queue tolerance, slot bidding, and intermodal substitution.

subUnits.name: Energy and bulk shippers
subUnits.description:
Bulk and energy cargoes faced their own decisions about draft, slot availability, and whether alternate routes would be more predictable.
- Role: Draft-sensitive canal demand
- Geographic anchor: Concentrated in queue areas and in the wider route calculations shaped by limited daily transits.
- Why it matters: They make clear that the canal's drought is not just a container story but a broader commodity-routing problem.
- What to watch: Draft limits, waiting cost, and alternative routes or terminals.

events.title: Low-water concerns move from forecast to operations
events.description: By early summer, reduced rainfall and reservoir stress had become an operational concern rather than a purely hydrologic warning.

events.title: Draft restrictions tighten
events.description: Restrictions on draft signaled that water scarcity was directly affecting the types of ships and loads the canal could efficiently handle.

events.title: Transit caps draw global attention
events.description: Limits on daily ship transits made the drought visible to global shipping markets and pushed carriers to reassess slot competition and queue tolerance.

events.title: Queues and booking competition peak
events.description: By late 2023, slot scarcity and queue build-up had turned the canal into a scheduling and bidding story as much as an infrastructure one.

events.title: Water-saving and booking adjustments continue
events.description: Operational changes continued into 2024 as the authority tried to match transit demand to a still-constrained water system.

events.title: Improving rains begin to ease the tightest restrictions
events.description: As water conditions improved, some pressure on the system eased, but the drought had already demonstrated how exposed global shipping is to the canal's freshwater balance.

meta.name: Gatun Lake
meta.description: The lake whose water level directly influences how many ships the canal can handle and under what draft limits.

meta.name: Miraflores and Pedro Miguel locks
meta.description: The Pacific-side lock complex where water use and ship movement become physically visible to anyone reading the canal as infrastructure.

meta.name: Atlantic queue area
meta.description: Holding area for ships waiting to enter from the Atlantic side of the canal system.

meta.name: Pacific queue area
meta.description: Holding area for ships waiting to enter from the Pacific side.

meta.name: Isthmus intermodal alternative
meta.description: Simplified line representing Panama's short intermodal alternatives that gain relevance when canal slots become harder to secure.
